Once gum tissue is lost, it does not grow back, so in order to restore a full, healthy gum line, we will need to perform what is called soft tissue grafting or gum grafting. After periodontal disease has been successfully treated, these enlarged pockets can actually increase a person’s chances of redeveloping the disease, and they can also noticeably affect the appearance of their smile. Pocket reduction surgery, as the name implies, is intended to make these areas much smaller, which provides both cosmetic and health benefits.

This will make them less likely to accumulate plaque over time, and therefore develop gum disease, in the future. There are two distinct kinds of periodontal disease. Scaling & Root Planing Protect Your Gums Scaling and root planing (SRP) is usually how we try to prevent the damage that makes all of the above procedures necessary. It’s our first line of defense against early-stage periodontal disease, and it’s basically a very deep cleaning that’s focused solely on the gums and the roots of the teeth. An ultrasonic scaler will be used to break up and clear away plaque built up around the gum line, and then the roots of the teeth will be gently smoothed out.

The areas between the teeth and gums tend to be the prime breeding ground for periodontal disease bacteria, so in order to eliminate it, these pockets need to be thoroughly cleaned.
